_comment: MULTI-TURN DIALOGUE EXERCISE (type: dialogue) — a scripted conversation where the learner takes turns responding. Currently 16 in the corpus; all learner turns use mode: mc. Use for one climactic exercise at the end of a lesson block, after words have been introduced individually. Each dialogue should test 3–6 words from vocabIds.
_prompt: Scene title shown as the exercise header. Keep short — 2–4 words. Required.
_sub: Subtitle. Usually 'Use what you know'. Required.
scene: You are walking with your Raksótha (grandfather) at the edge of the forest in early autumn. He stops beside a stand of trees and turns to you.
_scene: Narrative scene-setter shown to the learner before the conversation begins. 2–4 sentences. Ground the exchange in a specific, believable community moment. Use character names from the glossary where possible (Rokstén:ha, Raksótha, Akokstén:ha, etc.). Required.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He is greeting the leaf — addressing it directly. What does he mean by ohón:ta'?
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Ohón:ta' — leaf. In Kanien'kéha, it is not unusual to greet the plants and acknowledge them before taking from them. The Thanksgiving Address does this at scale — it thanks every living relative by name.
turns.wm: He said ohón:ta' — which word did you learn for this? Return to the vocabulary if you need to.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Raksótha
turns.line: Tsi iontatié:ras — kanónhsa n'í:'i. The forest — it is my home.
turns.gloss: The forest — it is home to me.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He says the forest is his home. How do you say 'the forest' in Kanien'kéha?
turns.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Tsi iontatié:ras — the forest. Literally: where they (the trees) stand together. The verb is doing the work — Kanien'kéha names places by what happens in them.
turns.wm: He said 'the forest is my home' — which of these words means forest?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He's asking where you came from. Tell him you came from home (kanónhsa).
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Non kanónhsa nontà:ke — I came from home. Non marks the origin (from); nontà:ke is the verb 'I came from'. wà:ke means 'I am going' — direction matters in Kanien'kéha.
turns.wm: He asked where you came from — you need the 'came from' verb (nontà:ke), not the 'going' verb (wà:ke). And non marks the origin.

_turns: Array of turn objects. Alternate other and learner turns, beginning with other. Each other turn: speaker, name, line (Kanien'kéha), gloss (English). Each learner turn: speaker (learner), instruction, mode (always mc), options (4), correct, cm, wm. Aim for 3–5 learner turns per dialogue.
closing: You moved through a real moment with your grandfather — greeting a plant, naming the forest, and saying where you came from. The direction verbs (wà:ke vs nontà:ke) are not interchangeable. Kanien'kéha encodes direction into the verb, not just the sentence.
_closing: Optional. Shown after the dialogue ends. 2–4 sentences summarising what the learner practiced and highlighting one key linguistic takeaway. Do not repeat the correct answers — give the learner something to carry forward.
dialect_note: In Akwesasne, Raksótha becomes Laksótha. All community forms accepted.
_dialect_note: Optional. Note any dialect variation relevant to this dialogue. Keep to one sentence.
_wids_tested: Optional but recommended. Flat list of all glossary IDs tested across the learner turns. Used for future analytics. Include every wid referenced in the correct answers.
